coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of polymer dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of Ponceau 4R, an additive color abuse in food
coredata.dc:creator: Sedighi, Vida
Faridbod, Farnoush
coredata.dc:description: Abusing organic dyes in industrial food products is an important issue in many countries. Rapid chemical sensing of these compounds can be of great importance during the industrial life of humans. In this work, we synthesized a new fluorescent polymer dot and successfully applied it as an optical probe for the detection of red color abuse in foodstuffs. Ponceau 4R is a red organic dye additive that is used in some foodstuffs such as tomato sauces or pastes. It is too hazardous to human health. Detection of such abusage is challenging. The development of π-conjugated polymer dots having a bright emission band at visible can be a promising probe for the detection of food color additives. A variety of methods and monomers were previously used for their synthesis. Here, the Suzuki Coupling method was employed. The limit of detection (LOD) of the method was obtained 16 nmol L−1 for the detection of Ponceau 4R.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ponceau 4R
Food color
Polymer dots
Fluorescence nanoprobe
Sensing color

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction between Ponceau 4R and the polymer dots optical nanoprobe
Optimization of parameters affecting the performance of the nanoprobe
The polymer dot nanosensor analytical performance in the determination of Ponceau 4R
Analysis of Ponceau 4R in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Research Council of the University of Tehran for the financial support of this work. Ponceau 4R (PubChem CID: 17467); Tetrabutylammonium bromide (PubChem CID: 74236), 1,6-Dibromohexane (PubChem CID: 12368); 2,7-Dibromofluorene (PubChem CID: 140073); Bis(pinacolato)diboron (PubChem CID: 2733548); [1,1-Bis(diphenylphosphino) ferrocene] dichloro palladium (II) (PubChem CID: 131726015); Potassium acetate (PubChem CID: 517044); dichloromethane (PubChem CID: 6344); dioxane (PubChem CID: 31275); Imipramine Bromide (PubChem CID: 65589); n-hexane (PubChem CID: 8058); N-bromosuccinimide (PubChem CID: 67184); Potassium carbonate (PubChem CID: 11430); Carmoisine (PubChem CID: 19118).
The authors thank the Research Council of the University of Tehran for the financial support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation steps to synthesize the polymer dots.
Characterization of the synthesized polymer dots DLS (A), Zeta potential (B), TEM and SEM image (F), and UV–vis and fluorescence spectra (G).

Fluorescence spectra of polymer dot in different concentrations of Ponceau 4R (A), calibration curve of Ponceau 4R (B), fluorescence spectra of polymer dot in different concentrations of Carmoisine (C) Calibration curve of Carmoisine (D).
Quantitative measurement of spiked Ponceau 4R in tomato paste.

row.entry: Water-soluble foods, such as fruit-flavored drinks, alcoholic drinks, jams, sugar confectionery, and sweets

head.ce:title: Synthesis of polymer dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of Ponceau 4R, an additive color abuse in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Kish International Campus, University of Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Kish International Campus, University of Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Department, Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Department, Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abusing organic dyes in industrial food products is an important issue in many countries. Rapid chemical sensing of these compounds can be of great importance during the industrial life of humans. In this work, we synthesized a new fluorescent polymer dot and successfully applied it as an optical probe for the detection of red color abuse in foodstuffs. Ponceau 4R is a red organic dye additive that is used in some foodstuffs such as tomato sauces or pastes. It is too hazardous to human health. Detection of such abusage is challenging. The development of π-conjugated polymer dots having a bright emission band at visible can be a promising probe for the detection of food color additives. A variety of methods and monomers were previously used for their synthesis. Here, the Suzuki Coupling method was employed. The limit of detection (LOD) of the method was obtained 16 nmol L−1 for the detection of Ponceau 4R.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Today’s urban life causes industrial foodstuffs to enter life more than ever. Economic considerations and competition to decrease the final price of a product extend the abuse of many chemicals during the production process. This issue has caused many organic compounds to enter our lives and gradually threaten our health. Synthetic food color additives, also called food chemical dyes, are one of the threats. They are generally synthesized from some toxic chemical compounds which are the origin of diseases such as cancer (Cetinkaya, & Yurtsever, 2021). Numerous researchers have studied the carcinogenic effects of these chemicals consumption. Today, there are more concerns because of increased public awareness about the effect of chemical food color additives (Ahmed, Al-Khalifa, Al-Nouri, & El-din, 2021). According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) statements, the Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) recommends that governments frequently supervise the consumption of each food additive color (König, 2015).

Ponceau 4R (E124) with the chemical name of trisodium (8Z)-7-oxo-8-[(4-sulfonatonaphthalen-1-yl) hydrazinylidene] naphthalene-1,3-disulfonate)) is a red azoic dye color food which is used as a synthetic additive color (Leulescu, Lacobescu, Bojan, & Rotaru, 2019), in desserts, cheeses, meats, preserved fruits, and sauces (Motarjemi, Moy, & Todd, 2014). Recently abuse of food color additives such as Ponceau 4R as fake tomato sauces has been rising. Since the finding of Ponceau 4R's chemical structure, it has been targeted by many analytical researchers over the years. There are various analytical techniques for the identification and detection of Ponceau 4R including chromatographic techniques (Minioti, Sakellariou, & Thomaidis, 2007), immunosorbent assay (Dong et al., 2015), electrochemical methods (Wang, Gao, Sun, & Zhao, 2015), and spectroscopic methods (Zhang et al., 2017; Turak, Dinc, Dulger, & Ozgur, 2014). Although these methods are very sensitive, some drawbacks include high cost, expert operatory, time-consuming, and solvent usage. Therefore, developing new simple, time-saving, cost-effective, and especially sensitive detection methods is always vital.

The development of nanotechnology introduced fluorescent nanoparticles as alternative materials for organic fluorophores, such as metal quantum dots (Tobias, & Jones, 2019), carbon quantum dots (Koutsogiannis, Thomou, Stamatis, Gournis, & Rudolf, 2020), some metal nano-oxides, nano-clusters (Bhattacharyya, & Mukherjee, 2018), and nano polymers (Adhikari, 2021). Fluorescent nanoparticles can be used in various areas such as medical sciences, chemical industries, food industries, monitoring of environmental pollutants, intelligent drug delivery, and so on. They have also been considered sensitive materials to identify different types of molecules and materials (Elmizadeh, Soleimani, Faridbod, & Bardajee, 2019; Mehrzad-Samarin, Faridbod, & Ganjali, 2019; Amiri, Faridbod, & Zoughi, 2021; Shahrivari, Faridbod, & Ganjali, 2018). Among them, metal quantum dots possessing narrow-emission bands, and highly adjustable wavelengths, suffer a lack of stability over time (Arquer et al., 2021). Moreover, their possible cytotoxicity is another drawback. Hence, researchers changed their trends to alternative materials such as carbon quantum dots (Ganguly, Das, Banerjee, & Das, 2019). They are biocompatible, easy to surface functionalization, low toxicity, and highly stable, although they have some disadvantages such as hydrophobicity, and instability in the dimensions of 10 nm (Arquer et al., 2021).

Recently polymer dots have received much attention due to their remarkable properties. When polymers change to the nanoscale, some physicochemical characteristics are changed (Xu, & Liu, 2021). Polymer dots are categorized as unconjugated, conjugated, and polymer electrolyte dots (Zhao, Liu, Song, Zhou, & Shan, 2019; Palani, & Karpagam, 2021). Among them, conjugated polymers (CPs) can be a good choice for making sensing probes because of their smaller, quicker, and more energy-efficient identification compounds (Chen, Hussain, Hao, Tian, & Gao, 2021). Also, they have high selectivity, adequate sensitivity, water solubility, low toxicity, and more efficiency in visible and near-infrared wavelengths (Guo, Ge, & Wang, 2018). Using fluorescent conjugated polymer is more useful and economical for an effective identification of chemical compounds abuses in food industries (Wang, Zhang, Bai, & Bao, 2020).

There are several food colorants that their consumption is forbidden nowadays because of their toxicity, side effects, and effect on the health and safety of human life in the long-term. It is clear that the measurement of food colorants amounts to finding the abuses or fraud in foodstuffs is consequential. Furthermore, these days consumers tend to use organic products with a lack of synthetic food colorants. Many researchers have reported methods for the detection of Ponceau 4R as a food colorant. Zhang et al. described the detection of Ponceau 4R using fluorescence of the graphene quantum dot with LOD 2.57 µg mL−1 (Zhang, Na, Jiang, Lou & Jinalso, 2016). Lelis et al. reported a surface plasmon resonance and fluorescent spectroscopic method for detecting the Ponceau 4R concentration from low amounts to 5.0 × 10−5 molL−1 (Lelis et al., 2017). A literature survey reveals that the detection of Ponceau 4R using fluorescence spectroscopy, especially using polymer dots as a sensing probe is very rare.

In this study, we synthesized a new conjugated polymer dot and introduced it as a novel fluorescent probe for the determination of the amount of chemical color additive, Ponceau 4R (Fig. 1 ) in some foodstuffs. Interaction between Ponceau 4R and the synthesized polymer dots causes a change in the fluorescent intensity of the polymer dots. This response is comparable to the responses of the probe to other food color additives such as Carmoisine (with a similar chemical structure), and natural Lycopene (natural red color) (Fig. 1).

Tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB), aqueous potassium hydroxide (KOH), 1,6-dibromohexane (Br (CH2)6Br), 2,7-dibromofluorene (C13H8Br2) were obtained from Merck Chemicals. They are used for the first stage of synthesizing polymer as row materials. Also, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) and aqueous HCl was used as solvent at this stage. Purification was done by column chromatography using n-hexane:CHCl3. Bis(pinacolato)diboron ((CH3)4C2O2B]2), potassium acetate (CH3COOK), [1,1-Bis(diphenylphosphino) ferrocene] dichloro palladium (II) (Pd(dppf)Cl2), were obtained for the second stage of synthesizing polymer. Dioxane and dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) was used as solvent at this stage. In addition, purification was done by column chromatography using n-hexane:CH2Cl2. Imipramine bromide, potassium carbonate (K2CO3), triphenylphosphine) palladium(II) Pd(PPh3)4 was obtained for the third stage of the polymer synthesis. Toluene and water were used as solvents in this stage. Imipramine and N-bromosuccinimide were purchased from a local pharmaceutical company in Iran. All solvents and reagents were bought from Merck Chemicals Co. and were used as received.

IR spectra were taken by PerkinElmer Spectrum Version 10.03.06 and 1HNMR spectra were taken by a Varian INOVA500 spectrometer. UV–Vis absorptions were recorded by a Perkin-Elmer (Lambda 25) UV–Vis spectrometer, and Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer luminescence spectrometer Fluorescence (LS50). DLS measurements were taken by a HORIBA 7100.

Compound A was synthesized from 1,6-dibromo hexane and 2,7-Dibromo-9H-fluorene as reported by Liu and Bazan (Liu, & Bazan, 2006). Tetrabutylammonium bromide (300 mg, 0.93 mmol) and aqueous potassium hydroxide (100 mL, 50%) were mixed and then 1,6-dibromohexane (22.6 g, 92.6 mmol) and 2,7-dibromofluorene (3.24 g, 10 mmol) were added in reflex condition at 75 °C. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 12 h. The synthesized compound was washed with 100 mL of CH2Cl2 three times and dried over MgSO4. For purifying the residue, silica gel column chromatography (n-hexane: CHCl3 9:1) was used to give compound A (5.0 g, 80% yield) as a light-yellow solid, Rf = 0.47.1; 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3): δ (ppm) 7.2–7.5 (m, 6H), 3.11 (t, 4H), 1.74 (t, 4H), 1.4 (m, 4H), 1.1 (m, 8H), 0.35 (m, 4H); FT-IR spectrum of compound A showed absorption bands at 2922 (sp2 stretching, CH Alkene), 2872 (sp3 stretching, CH Alkans), 1598 (aromatic stretching), 1420 (–CH2 Bending); 576 (C-Br bending) cm−1; absorption λmax: 240 nm (CH2Cl2 solution).

In this stage, one of the monomers must be prepared for the Suzuki coupling reaction with Bis(pinacolato)diboron. The reaction was set by co-mixing the compounds and regents in a 250-mL round-bottom flask while stirring. Compound A (10 mmol), potassium acetate (24 mmol) and [1,1-bis(diphenylphosphino)ferrocene]dichloro palladium (II) (0.5 g) were mixed well. The reaction was degassed by Schleck line condition and done at 85 °C for one day. The synthesized compound was washed with CH2Cl2 and water three times and dried over MgSO4 (Hayashi et al., 2017). For purifying the residue, silica gel column chromatography (n-hexane: CH2Cl2 2:1) was used to give compound B (4.2 g, 59% yield) as a white solid; 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3): δ (ppm) 7.80–7.70 (m, 6H), 3.28–3.23 (t, J = 6.8 Hz, 4H), 2.01–1.98 (q, J = 4.0 Hz, 4H), 1.63–1.56 (q, J = 7.2 Hz, 4H), 1.40 (s, 24H), 1.16–1.12 (q, 4H), 1.05–1.01 (q, 4H), 0.53 (m, 4H); FT-IR spectrum of compound B showed absorption bands at 2950 (=CH sp2 stretching, Alkene), 2820 (CH sp3 stretching, Alkanes), 1710 (CC stretching), 1575 (aromatic stretching), 1465 (CH2 bending), 1380 (B-O stretching), 650–753 (C-B bending), 490 (C-Br stretching) cm−1; absorption λmax: 231, 276 nm (CH2Cl2 solution).

To prepare one of the monomers, 2,8-dibromo-10,11-dihydro-5-[3-(dimethylamino) propyl] -5H-dibenzo[b,f]azepine must be conducted with Br, so 2,8-dibromo-10,11-dihydro-5-[3-(dimethylamino) propyl]-5H-dibenzo[b,f]azepine (5 mmol) was dissolved in a mixture of CH2Cl2 and CHCl3 (50:50 v/v, 200 mL), for reduction temperature, all materials were put into an ice bath. After that, N-bromosuccinimide (11.5 mmol) was added to the mixture. The reaction mixture has been stirred overnight, and after that, the temperature of the reaction mixture has been reached at room temperature. The reaction solution was extracted by CHCl3 (Ntrallou, Gika & Tsochatzis, 2020). After the evaporation of the solvent, the product was purified by re-precipitation from n-hexane to give 2.22 g (4.7 mmol) of 3-(2,8-dibromo-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[b,f]azepin-5-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropan-1-amine (Fig. 2 A) as a brown solid; 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3): δ (ppm) 7.4–7.1 (m, 4H), 7.08 (d, 2H, J = 8.3 Hz), 3.71 (t, 2H, J = 6.3 Hz), 3.08 (s, 4H), 2.5 (t, 2H, J = 7.5 Hz), 2.46 (d, 6H, J = 4.7 Hz), 2.2 (s, 6H, NMe3), 1.77–1.83 (m, 2H); FT-IR spectrum of this compound showed absorption bands at 3160.55 (=CH sp2 stretching), 2953.40 (CH sp3 stretching), 1707.40 (N in ring stretching), 1478.79; 1293.59; 1370.83 (aromatic stretching), 1189 (CN bending), 1002.97 (aromatic bending), 819.85; 933.59; 3450.63 (NH stretching), 644.16 (C-Br bending) cm−1; absorption λmax: 274 nm (water solution).

Conjugated polymers were prepared as Suzuki Coupling in previous papers. In brief, freshly synthesized two monomers must be conducted together. For this gold, the reaction was set by commixing the compounds and regents up in a 250-mL round-bottom flask with a stir bar. Compound B (10 mmol), 3-(2,8-dibromo-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[b,f]azepin-5-yl)-N, N-dimethylpropan-1-amine (24 mmol), potassium carbonate, and triphenylphosphine) palladium (0) (Pd(PPh3)4) (0.5 g) were added. The reaction was degassed by Schleck line condition and done under 85 °C for five days. The synthesized compound was washed with hot methanol and acetone (Ntrallou, Gika, & Tsochatzis, 2020). The polymer was dried at 40 °C in a vacuum oven for 24 h. All steps are shown in Fig. 2B. The yield was 49% as an orange polymeric product. 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3): δ (ppm) 7.8–7.3 (m,4 rings), 3.9 (t, 2H), 3.1 (s, 2H), 2.2 (t, 2H, J = 6.5 Hz), 2.1 (s, 6H, NMe3), 2.0 (2H), 1.9 (2H), 0.4–0.6 (Alkane Unit); FT-IR spectrum of final compound showed absorption bands at 3450 (NH stretching), 3160 (CH sp2 stretching) 2953.0 (CH sp3 stretching), 1707.0 (CC stretching), 1478.09 (aromatic stretching), 1189.9 (CN bending), 644.06 (C-Br bending) cm−1; TGA gives Td5 = 300 °C and Td50 = 350 °C in nitrogen; absorption λmax: 420 nm (CH2Cl2/Water 20:80 solution).

To prepare the polymer dots, the nanoprecipitation method was used. First of all, 0.2 mg polymer was solved in 2 mL THF then 50 µL PEG was added to this solution. All of this material was added to 10 mL water slowly under ultrasonic conditions. Continuing this process; filtration was done by a 45-µm filter (Hornig, Heinze, Becerbc, & Schubert, 2009; Adhikari, 2021).

Samples of tomato paste and fruit leather were bought from a local supermarket in Tehran (Iran). All sample preparation for these materials was done according to the standard method. 5 mg tomato paste was weighed and dissolved in 5 mL NaOH (10−3 molL−1) then 5 mL water was added. The tube was covered and vortexed for one minute. The tube content was centrifuged at 4000 rpm (or 2270 × g) for 5 min. The supernatant was then separated by filter paper. An adequate volume of this solution was applied for Ponceau 4R analysis (Ntrallou, Gika, & Tsochatzis, 2020).

Fig. 3 A shows the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) result of the synthesized polymer dots. The size of polymer dots was smaller than 50 nm (Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows the result of the zeta potential study of the synthesized conjugated polymer nanoparticles. The stability of the dispersed polymer nanoparticle can be determined by zeta potential. This technique can measure the surface charge on the colloidal nanoparticles (Zhao, Uzunoff, Green, & Rakovich, 2023). The surface charge on the conjugated polymer molecules and the intermolecular forces between the nanoparticles is the main reason for the stability of nanopolymers. Therefore, a higher surface charge of the nanoparticles causes their more stability. As previously reported (Zhao, Uzunoff, Green, & Rakovich, 2023), zeta potential values of greater than 30–40 mV can be an indicator of good electrostatic stability of nanoparticles. Here, it was obtained −43.5 mV. Furthermore, this value of zeta potential can prevent nanoparticle agglomeration and aggregation (Gabriel, Edward, Jessica, Laurence, & Simon, 2021). Preventing agglomeration can enhance the stability of nanoparticles which is important for the potential of fluorescence polymer dots probe as a probe in the solution. Unfortunately, the stability of nanoparticles over time is limited due to aggregation. Using the PEGylation method for nanoparticle synthesizing can reduce agglomeration and improve stability (Cauda, Schlossbauer & Bein, 2010).

To provide information about the morphology and topography of the nanoparticles, SEM, and TEM were carried out (Fig. 3C). Fig. 1D shows the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrum of the synthesized polymer dots. Excitation was done at 440 nm wavelength for fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between Ponceau 4R and the polymer dots optical nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching of polymer dots occurs in the presence of the Ponceau 4R molecule. The UV–vis absorption of Ponceau 4R (λmax at 510 nm) and fluorescence emission spectrum of polymer dots (λex at 440 nm and λem at 480 nm) has a rather overlap but interfering studied of similar structure and other red color compounds showed that there is a chemical interaction between polymer dots and Ponceau 4R compound, which assumes that it is a kind molecular interaction such as hydrogen bonding or π-π interactions or covalent binding. There are three different mechanisms when an analyte is added to a fluorescent probe and all of these mechanisms are likely to occur and cause to change in the fluorescence spectrum. Firstly, when the analyte is added to a fluorescence probe, the intensity will be decreased, and can be pointed to quenching or turning off fluorescence; secondly, when the analyte is added to a fluorescent probe, the intensity will increase which can be referred to the enhancement or turning on the fluorescence; third one is when a shift in the wavelength maximum occurs. The quenching mechanism has been widely investigated and categorized into two general groups, dynamic quenching, and static quenching. Dynamic quenching has several subdivisions that depend on the distance between the fluorophore and the quencher (such as FRET, PET, and Dexter). Static quenching occurs when a fluorophore forms a non-fluorescent complex with a quencher and the product is no longer excitable (Tanwar, Parui, Garai, Chanu, & Iyer, 2022). Here, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching could be a kind of static quenching. The quantified fluorescence quenching of polymer dot can be defined by Stern-Volmer, equation (1):
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]

In the Stern-Volmer equation, F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of a quencher and F0 is the lack of a quencher. Ksv is defined as the quenching constant, and [Q] is the quencher concentration.

In this study, when the quencher was added to the polymer dots probe, the intensity was decreased. The reduction of the number of free fluorophore molecules available for excitation after complex formation in the ground state causes such quenching. It can be assumed that hydrogen bonding between the phenolic OH group on the analyte and amine groups of polymer dots causes these molecules to get close to each other and then phenolic OH reacts with the alkyl-Br moiety of the polymer dot (O-alkylation or C-alkylation in protic solvents like water) (Carey, & Sundberg, 2007). Such interaction causes a static quenching between the analyte as a quencher and polymer dot molecules as a fluorophore. It should be noted that this is just a hypothesis and for a better understanding of the mechanism more studies should be done which is out of the scope of this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the performance of the nanoprobe

ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect on the polymer dot fluorescence intensity was observed over a range of 5.0–9.0. The concentration of Ponceau 4R was similar for all of the measurements. Ponceau 4R response was measured in different pH ranges. The reaction intensity of the polymer dot was decreased in basic pH (pH = 9) and acidic pH (pH = 5). According to the proposed response mechanism discussed above, in acidic pH quenching constant (Ksv) is decreased, this might be an effect of the positive charge in the polymer dot surface which reduces the interaction of polymer dots and Ponceau 4R and inhibits the o or c-alkylation reaction. In alkaline pH, the quenching constant (Ksv) is increased lightly, this may be an effect of competing OH− with the phenolic group of Ponceau 4R. Moreover, hydrogen bonding was decreased with the polymer dot and Ponceau 4R in acidic pH. Based on the obtained results presented in Fig. 1S, neutral pH was selected as optimal for measurement during different experiments. The fluorescence measurements of each pH of Ponceau 4R were repeated in four different concentrations from 30 to 130 nmolL−1. Increasing the concentration showed a significant reduction in the response intensity of the polymer dot in basic and acidic pH compared to the neutral pH (Fig. 1S).

Monitoring of the effective interaction of Ponceau 4R with the polymer dots was done by studying the quenching of the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence spectra of polymer dots were recorded in optimized conditions (at a pH of 7.0, a concentration of 100 nmolL−1, and a temperature of 25 ± 1 0C). The time was studied over the range of 0 to 20 min. maximum quenching observed after 15 min (Fig. 2S). This time was also confirmed a static quenching. It means it takes time for the fluorescent polymer dot to interact with the analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The polymer dot nanosensor analytical performance in the determination of Ponceau 4R
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the polymer dot were recorded in the presence of the different concentrations of Ponceau 4R as calibration series (Fig. 4 A).

Liner ranges from 30 to 130 nmolL−1 and LOD of 16 nmolL−1 was obtained for Ponceau 4R (Fig. 4B). The presented results were the mean of the five replicate measurements with a relative standard deviation of 3.2 that confirms the excellent reproducibility of the method. The solution of the polymer dot can be stable for about three months if kept at 4 °C with a response loss of less than 6.7%. The reproducibility of the nanosensor was tested by producing three similar batches of nanomaterial. The RSD of the response was less than 7.4%.
To study the selectivity of the proposed nanosensor which is designed based on the conjugated polymer dot, another similar structure compound, Carmoisine (Fig. 1B) was tested.

Carmoisine does not have any fluorescent emission at 480 nm and causes fluorescence quenching. Because of the similar structure and excitation of Carmoisine and Ponceau 4R, both compounds can quench the fluorescence intensity of the polymer dots (Dong et al., 2015).

Probably the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be defined as a kind of interaction between polymer dot and Carmoisine, like what is described in the case of Ponceau 4R. Ponceau 4R, however, shows a more sensitive response compared to Carmoisine. Also, we did not observe any significant quenching in the polymer dot fluorescence in the presence of tomato juice or sauce. It would be noted that the color of the tomato is related to natural lycopene content (Fig. 1C).

According to this result, a combination of the quencher and food color additives such as Ponceau 4R and Carmoisine because of hydrogen bonding ability and phenolic group interaction with alkyl-Br moiety of the polymer dots results in a turn-off response. In addition, because of the formation of a huge complex between Ponceau 4R, Carmoisine, and conjugated polymer dots, the degree of order decreases. Crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) is a known result of aggregated or cross-linked polymer structures. Covalent or non-covalent bonds can cause crosslinking-enhanced emission (CEE) (Huy et al., 2022). The bonding causes stable interaction that causes stability and prevents CEE and when Ponceau 4R or Carmoisine is added to a fluorescent polymer dot, the above mechanism may be activated, hence, the intensity decreases which is observed as turn-off fluorescence or quenching. For such a mechanism, the analyte must be able to interact with the fluorescence-based sensor (polymer dots) by covalent or non-covalent bonds (Skorjanc, Shetty, & Valant, 2021). Ponceau 4R and Carmoisine as analytes can react to polymer dots as fluorescence-based sensors while Lycopene because of the structure cannot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ponceau 4R in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the presence of Ponceau 4R in real samples using polymer dots as a fluorescence probe, tomato paste was purchased from a supermarket. Additionally, to verify the efficacy of the nanoprobe, tomato samples were also used as free of Ponceau 4R food colorant. The sample, tomato paste, and tomato juice were prepared as described above.

To verify the performance of the proposed nanoprobe a known concentration of Ponceau 4R was spiked into the tomato sample. Then, by standard addition methods, the samples were analyzed. Table 1 shows the recovered concentration of the spiked samples using the nanosensor.

Determining the abuse of toxic synthetic colorants in food, and supporting consumers’ health, is not only challenging but also complex. There are various analytical techniques to determine synthetic food colorants in foodstuffs. They include spectrophotometry using fluorescence quenching sensor probes including quantum dots and polymer dots, capillary electrophoresis, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS), and thin layer chromatography (TLC). Food colorant compounds are absorbed extremely in UV and visible regions so using simple spectrophotometry is sufficient and suitable for high concentration level detection. Using other techniques for detecting the low amount of them such as MS and HPLC, although they have low excellent sensitivity, they are too expensive, time-consuming, and need expert operators. Capillary electrophoresis (CE) is also used for detecting small molecules and it is used for detecting food colorants rarely (Ntrallou, Gika, & Tsochatzis, 2020). Also, electrochemical methods using nanoparticles, as electrode surface modifiers, were applied for Ponceau 4R determination in fruit juice samples. Although comparing all these techniques with different advantages and disadvantages may be are not reasonable, using fluorescence quenching chemosensor or probes are logical.

Researchers investigated and detected Ponceau 4R by using a fluorescence quenching of quantum dots (Zhang, Na, Jiang, Lou, & Jin 2016). They used CdTe as a quantum dot for the detection of additive food color. According to their research, the linear range was mentioned as 2.5 to 25 µg L−1. Although this method has better results compared with other methods reported before (Gunjal et al., 2021), using a polymer dot as a fluorescence probe showed a superior result. Because of decreasing concentration fluoresce probe and increasing LOD for the detection of Ponceau 4R, using conjugated polymer dots compared with mentioned methods (Table 2 ).

As seen in Table 2, comparing other fluorescent probes, concerning heavy metal-based quantum dots, and GQDs, to polymer dots, exhibits several advantages including high brightness and biocompatibility and lower LOD. Another benefit of polymer dots is their absorption wavelength. They can enhance absorption wavelength up to 700 nm by changing the type of monomers. Our study showed a LOD of 16 nmolL−1 for the determination of Ponceau 4R.

In this study, we successfully designed a new conjugated polymer dot fluorescent probe for the detection of Ponceau 4R abuse. This food color additive has potential risks to human health. It can cause many undesirable side effects including the appearance of allergies, asthma, and attention hyperactivity disorder. Therefore, sensitive detection of Ponceau 4R in foodstuffs as an abused additive is of great importance. Researchers attend to analyses of synthetic food colorants using various analytical methodologies. Although MS, HPLC, capillary electrophoresis and TLC are used for analyzing Ponceau 4R, fluorescence spectroscopy can be a simple alternative detection method. When this technique is coupled with nanochemistry, they can offer a new approach. Among the fluorescent nanoparticles as sensing probes, polymer dots are one of the substantial and innovative nanoparticles that have the potential to be more investigated as fluorescent detection probes. Here, we reported a simple method for the detection of Ponceau 4R as a synthetic food colorant using fluorescent polymer dots. Selecting other monomers in synthesizing polymer dots offers a variety of nanomaterials that have the potential to detect other food colorants. This method is more rapid and inexpensive than other complex methods and can be easily used to ensure food safety. The demonstration of new highly sensitive and selective detection of Ponceau 4R as an additive food color by taking a new fluorescence nanoprobe based on new conjugated polymer dots is one of the rapid analytical methods. The result showed that the fluorescence intensity of the conjugated polymer dot was decreased in a linear concentration range from 30 to 130n molL−1 with a detection limit of 16 nmolL−1. Furthermore, the conjugated polymer dot can be used for Carmasine which has a similar structure to Ponceau 4R but with lower sensitivity. The sensing mechanism is based on the fluorescence static quenching of conjugated polymer dots in interaction with Ponceau 4R additive food color (a turn-off chemosensor). The method was successfully used in analyzing Penceau 4R in tomato juices and paste samples.
Vida Sedighi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Farnoush Faridbod: Conceptualization, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors thank the Research Council of the University of Tehran for the financial support of this work.

Ponceau 4R (PubChem CID: 17467); Tetrabutylammonium bromide (PubChem CID: 74236), 1,6-Dibromohexane (PubChem CID: 12368); 2,7-Dibromofluorene (PubChem CID: 140073); Bis(pinacolato)diboron (PubChem CID: 2733548); [1,1-Bis(diphenylphosphino) ferrocene] dichloro palladium (II) (PubChem CID: 131726015); Potassium acetate (PubChem CID: 517044); dichloromethane (PubChem CID: 6344); dioxane (PubChem CID: 31275); Imipramine Bromide (PubChem CID: 65589); n-hexane (PubChem CID: 8058); N-bromosuccinimide (PubChem CID: 67184); Potassium carbonate (PubChem CID: 11430); Carmoisine (PubChem CID: 19118).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137402.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
